# Imajin Network (MJN Protocol)
# llms-full.txt — Complete technical reference for AI consumption
# v0.8 · July 2026
# Ryan Veteze · ryan@imajin.ai · github.com/ima-jin/imajin-ai
#
# This document is the single source of truth for the Imajin architecture.
# An LLM should be able to build a working client from this document alone.
# A human should be able to understand why every design choice was made.

---

## 1. What This Is

**For 2,000 years the money was in the lie.** Information asymmetry — I know what you
don't, I hide it, I profit from the gap — is the business model under advertising,
finance, platforms, and supply chains. Imajin is the **light web**: a sovereign,
auditable layer where the signed record IS the value, so honesty becomes the profitable
move. Be honest. Make money.

You do a human thing — send a voice note, take a photo, drop a file. An agent infers
what you meant, acts on your behalf, asks before anything crosses a boundary, and signs
a record of what it did. The machinery recedes; the proof remains.

Imajin is a cryptographic operating system. One kernel, a federated userspace, and four
infrastructure layers that make user-authored actions verifiable and relationships bilateral.
What is user-signed, platform-signed, or merely logged is spelled out per service — not
everything is independently verifiable, and the design is explicit about which is which.

Without the chains, each service is a dumb CRUD app. With the chains, they form a sovereign
operating system where identity, attribution, trust, and settlement are structural properties —
not features bolted on after the fact.

Federation is progressive: today this runs as a single operator's node. Cross-node trust and
DID resolution at scale are roadmap milestones, not shipped guarantees — the "sovereign"
property is real for a single node and a stated direction for the network, not a finished
multi-node claim. See SECURITY.md for the honest list of current gaps.

The kernel (`apps/kernel`) consolidates auth, payments, messaging, media, trust graph,
broker, MCP connector, calendar, and registry into one Next.js application. One domain,
one cookie, one build. Userspace apps (events, market, learn, coffee, links, dykil) are
independent, replaceable, and disposable. They inherit the full trust graph by importing
`@imajin/auth`. The value is never in the app layer — it's in the substrate underneath.

No service mesh. No orchestration framework. No API gateway. No Kubernetes. No Docker.
Just Next.js apps behind Caddy on a single server with pm2.

**Build stats (July 2026):**
~101,500 lines of TypeScript across 22 shared packages. 2,844 commits in 153 days.
70 database migrations. One developer with AI agents. The constraint is intentional —
if the architecture can't be maintained by a small team, it's the wrong architecture.

---

## 2. Architecture: Kernel + Bus + Vault + Broker

Four infrastructure layers. The kernel runs the substrate. The bus moves events. The vault
encrypts data. The broker mediates access. Everything else is userspace.

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Userspace (disposable, replaceable)                           │
│  events · market · learn · coffee · links · dykil              │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Kernel                                                        │
│  auth · pay · chat · media · profile · connections · registry  │
│  calendar · mcp · oauth · supply · inference                   │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Bus ─ reactor chains (side effects are composable)            │
│  Vault ─ encrypted store (AES-256-GCM, node-sealed)            │
│  Broker ─ consent gate (data released only with permission)    │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│  Cryptographic Substrate                                       │
│  Ed25519 · CID · dag-cbor · DFOS chains · .fair manifests      │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Why one kernel, not 15 services:**
We started with 15. CORS, cookie forwarding, session validation round-trips, and deploy
coordination made it worse than a monolith without any of the benefits. The kernel is one
Next.js app with route prefixes. One cookie, one session, no inter-service trust assumptions.
Userspace apps stay independent because they're genuinely different domains (events vs market
vs learn). The kernel stays unified because auth, pay, chat, and media are all facets of the
same identity substrate.

### Kernel Route Map

| Route prefix | What it does |
|--------------|----------------|
| `/auth/*` | DIDs, sessions, attestations, challenges, registration, agents, apps |
| `/pay/*` | Stripe checkout, settlement, .fair splits, MJNx ledger |
| `/chat/*` | DID-based messaging, WebSocket tokens, conversations |
| `/media/*` | Asset storage, .fair sidecars, CID versioning, scope-manifest projection |
| `/profile/*` | Public profiles, handles, presence |
| `/connections/*` | Trust graph, pods, invites |
| `/registry/*` | Node federation, app manifests, DFOS relay |
| `/input/*` | Voice transcription, file processing |
| `/calendar/*` | Scheduling, broker-gated availability, consent-scoped entries |
| `/mcp/*` | Sovereign connector door (OAuth 2.1 + DCR, per-DID scopes, JSON-RPC) |
| `/oauth/*` | Authorization server for connectors (authorize, token, register) |
| `/supply/*` | Supply domain — lots, chains, recorder |
| `/inference/*` | Gesture capture, intention inference, vocabulary-mounted routing |
| `/broker/*` | HTTP broker API — consent grant CRUD, field-level selective disclosure |

Chat's WebSocket server (`ws-server.js`) runs as a separate process. Caddy routes `/chat/ws` to it.

---

## 3. Identity

Every entity in the system — person, agent, device, business, community — is a DID with an
Ed25519 keypair. The identity model is designed so that everyone starts with nothing (just an
email) and accumulates trust through real actions. No admin grants. No KYC gates. You earn
standing by doing things that get attested.

### DID Format

- **Soft DID**: `did:imajin:<nanoid(44)>` — random stable identifier minted at email verification. No keypair. Cannot sign. A placeholder that can be upgraded.
- **Hard DID**: `did:imajin:<sha256(Ed25519_pubkey)>` — deterministically derived from the Ed25519 public key. Cryptographically self-certifying. Verifiable without Imajin's server.

Conversation DIDs (chat):
- DM: `did:imajin:dm:<sha256(sorted_member_dids)>.slice(0,16)` — deterministic from participants
- Group: `did:imajin:group:<sha256(sorted_members).slice(0,16)>` — deterministic from sorted member DIDs
- Event chat: `did:imajin:event:<eventId>` or `did:imajin:evt_<eventId>`

### Five Standing Tiers

Tier progression: `soft → preliminary → established → steward → operator`

- **soft**: Email-verified only. No keypair. Can buy tickets, enroll in courses. Cannot send messages, sign attestations, or create listings.
- **preliminary**: Ed25519 keypair + invite code. Full trust graph access. Can send messages, create events and listings, issue attestations.
- **established**: Earned through attestation history. Can vouch for others, invite new members.
- **steward**: Trusted community steward. Elevated governance rights.
- **operator**: Platform operator. Full access.

Helper functions from `@imajin/auth`:
```typescript
import { isVerifiedTier, isEstablishedTier, isStewardTier, isOperatorTier } from '@imajin/auth';
// isVerifiedTier: preliminary+   isEstablishedTier: established+
// isStewardTier: steward+        isOperatorTier: operator only
```

### Four Identity Scopes

- **actor**: One DID, one keypair. Subtypes: `human`, `agent`, `device`, `presence`, `org`, `service`, `event`.
- **family**: Intimate shared trust. Shared resources, delegated authority.
- **community**: Shared practice. Quorum-governed, contribution-weighted.
- **business**: Structured entity. Roles, delegation chains. Cannot initiate connections — all outreach is consent-gated.

### Group Membership (identity_members)

Group identities (communities, businesses, families) link to member DIDs with roles:
`owner | admin | maintainer | agent | member`. Same table handles human membership in a
business, agent delegation to a principal, and device registration to an owner. One primitive,
many patterns.

### Act-As Delegation

Two delegation mechanisms:

1. **X-Acting-As** — group identity impersonation. A controller (owner/admin/maintainer/agent) of a group DID can act on its behalf:
```typescript
// In requireAuth — validated server-side
const actingAs = request.headers.get("x-acting-as");
// Calls: GET /auth/api/groups/{groupDid}/controllers/{callerDid}
// Returns: { valid: true, role: 'admin', allowedServices: ['events', 'market'] }
```

2. **X-Acting-For** — agent delegation. An agent DID can act for its principal:
```typescript
const actingFor = request.headers.get("x-acting-for");
// Validates that caller has role='agent' for the target identity
```

### Actor-DID Convention for External Integrations

External connectors (GitHub, Discord, QuickBooks) are registered as first-class **actor DIDs**
via protocol adapters. Each connector is a `did:imajin:*-connector` with its own identity row
and scope vocabulary. It authenticates as itself and acts `onBehalfOf` the human's DID using
`X-Acting-For`. Work produced by a connector (issues opened, invoices created) is owner-pinned
to the human's DID — authorship and provenance accrue to the human, not the connector.

### Lazy Minting

DIDs are minted on first use. No batch import. No onboarding step. No registration form.

An integration partner calls `POST /registry/identity` the first time it references an external
entity → DID minted under the partner's namespace. A buyer checks out → soft DID created from
their email at checkout time.

The pattern is always the same: get-or-create on first reference. The DID is the anchor;
properties accrue over time as interactions happen.

```
POST /auth/api/onboard
{ "email": "user@example.com", "name": "Jane", "redirectUrl": "...", "context": "buy a ticket" }
→ { sent: true }
// User clicks email link → soft DID created, session cookie set, redirect
```

### DFOS Anchoring

Every `did:imajin` can be anchored to a DFOS proof chain. Same Ed25519 keypair produces both `did:imajin:xxx` and `did:dfos:xxx` — byte-compatible, same curve.

```typescript
import { createIdentityChain, verifyChain, DFOS_DID_PREFIX } from '@imajin/dfos';

// Create a DFOS chain from an Imajin keypair
const { did, log, operationCID } = await createIdentityChain({
  privateKeyHex: '64-char-hex...',
  publicKeyHex: '64-char-hex...',
});
// Returns: did = 'did:dfos:...', log = [jwsToken]

// Verify a chain
const verified = await verifyChain(log);
// Returns: VerifiedIdentity with did, authKeys, assertKeys, controllerKeys
```

Chain verification endpoint:
```
GET /auth/api/identity/{did}/verify
→ { hasDfosChain: true, chainValid: true, chainLength: 3, dbConsistent: true }
```

---

## 4. Authentication

All auth flows produce the same session cookie (`imajin_session` in prod, `imajin_session_dev` in dev). Cookie attributes: `HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Lax; Domain=.imajin.ai` — valid across all `*.imajin.ai` services.

### Flow 1: Soft DID (Email / Magic Link)

**Direct soft session:**
```
POST /auth/api/session/soft
{ "email": "user@example.com", "name": "Jane" }
→ { "did": "did:imajin:abc...", "handle": null, "type": "human", "name": "Jane", "tier": "soft" }
```

**OnboardGate (email verification with proof):**
```
POST /auth/api/onboard
{ "email": "user@example.com", "name": "Jane", "redirectUrl": "...", "context": "buy a ticket" }
→ { "sent": true }
```
Token TTL: 15 minutes. Grace window for email scanners: 60 seconds.

**Magic link re-auth (soft DIDs only):**
```
POST /auth/api/onboard
{ "email": "user@example.com", "mode": "login", "wantPolling": true }
→ { "sent": true, "pollHandle": "..." }
```
Poll: `GET /auth/api/onboard/poll?handle=...` → claim via `POST /auth/api/onboard/claim`

### Flow 2: Challenge-Response (Ed25519 Keypair)

```
POST /auth/api/challenge
{ "handle": "alice" }  // or { "did": "did:imajin:..." }
→ { "challengeId": "chl_xxx", "challenge": "hex...", "expiresAt": "..." }
```
Challenge TTL: 5 minutes.

```
POST /auth/api/authenticate
{ "challengeId": "chl_xxx", "signature": "128-char-hex-ed25519-sig", "dfosChain": { ... } }
→ { "did": "did:imajin:...", "handle": "alice", "type": "human", "name": "Alice" }
```
Signature: Ed25519 sign of the raw `challenge` hex string using your private key.

### Flow 3: Registration (New Keypair)

Derive DID client-side:
```typescript
const did = "did:imajin:" + sha256(publicKey_bytes).hex();
```

```
POST /auth/api/register
{
  "publicKey": "64-char-hex...",
  "handle": "alice",
  "name": "Alice",
  "type": "human",
  "signature": "128-char-hex...",
  "inviteCode": "abc123...",
  "email": "alice@example.com",
  "dfosChain": { ... }
}
→ { "did": "did:imajin:...", "handle": "alice", "type": "human", "created": true }
```

Type values: `human | agent | presence | org | device | service | event`
Human registration requires an invite code. Service accounts bypass the invite gate.

### Auth Middleware Hierarchy

`@imajin/auth` exposes these guards:

| Function | Tier accepted | Use case |
|----------|--------------|----------|
| `requireAuth(request, options?)` | soft+ | Most endpoints |
| `requireHardDID(request)` | preliminary+ | Signing, trust-gated actions |
| `requireEstablishedDID(request)` | established+ | Vouching, advanced trust ops |

All return `{ identity: Identity } | { error: string, status: number }`.

`Identity` shape:
```typescript
interface Identity {
  id: string;                    // DID
  scope: string;                 // 'actor' | 'family' | 'community' | 'business'
  subtype?: string;              // 'human' | 'agent' | 'device' | etc.
  name?: string;
  handle?: string;
  email?: string | null;
  tier?: "soft" | "preliminary" | "established" | "steward" | "operator";
  chainVerified?: boolean;
  actingAs?: string;             // group DID being impersonated
  actingAsRole?: string;         // role in that group
  actingAsServices?: string[];   // restricted service list (undefined = full)
  actingFor?: string;            // DID the agent is delegated to
  actingForRole?: string;        // always 'agent' for now
}
```

In API routes:
```typescript
const authResult = await requireAuth(request, { verifyChain: true, service: 'events' });
if ('error' in authResult) {
  return NextResponse.json({ error: authResult.error }, { status: authResult.status });
}
const { identity } = authResult;
```

### App Authentication (Federated Services)

Userspace apps authenticate via `requireAppAuth()` using attestation-based consent:

```typescript
// Client sends:
X-App-DID: did:imajin:app_xxx
X-App-Authorization: att_xxx

// Server validates:
const result = await requireAppAuth(request, { scope: 'events:read' });
if ('error' in result) { /* 401/403 */ }
const { appDid, userDid, scopes, attestationId } = result.appAuth;
```

The attestation must be type `app.authorized`, issued by the user, subject = app DID, payload containing approved scopes. Validated at `/auth/api/apps/validate` (internal endpoint, Bearer `ATTESTATION_INTERNAL_API_KEY`).

### Session Validation

```
GET /auth/api/session  (with Cookie: imajin_session=...)
→ { did, handle, type, name, scope, subtype, tier, chainVerified }
```

Server components use `getSession()` from `@imajin/auth`:
```typescript
import { getSession } from '@imajin/auth';
const identity = await getSession({ service: 'events' });
// Reads from next/headers cookie store, validates acting-as server-side
```

### Bearer Tokens (Machine Clients)

```
Authorization: Bearer <token>
```

Validated via `POST /auth/api/validate`:
```
POST /auth/api/validate
{ "token": "imajin_tok_xxx" }
→ { valid: true, identity: { id, type, name, ... } }
```

---

## 5. Event Bus

The event bus (`@imajin/bus`) is the nervous system. Side effects are the hard part of any
system — "after payment succeeds, create an attestation, credit MJN tokens, settle the .fair
manifest, and notify the seller." Without the bus, that logic lives in webhook handlers as
a fragile imperative sequence. With the bus, it's a composable reactor chain: each step is
independent, configurable, and replaceable.

Services publish events. Reactor chains handle everything that happens next.

### Core Pattern

```typescript
import { publish } from '@imajin/bus';

await publish('listing.purchased', {
  issuer: buyerDid,
  subject: sellerDid,
  scope: 'market',
  payload: {
    context_id: listingId,
    context_type: 'market',
    amount: 15000,               // cents
    currency: 'CAD',
    fairManifest: { ... },       // .fair fee manifest
    funded: true,
    funded_provider: 'stripe',
    buyerDid,
    metadata: { listingId },
    interestDids: [buyerDid, sellerDid],
  },
});
```

The bus runs a reactor chain for the event type. Each reactor is fire-and-forget by default (`await: false`), or blocking (`await: true`) for ordering-sensitive operations like settlement.

### BusEvent Shape

```typescript
interface BusEvent {
  type: string;              // 'listing.purchased', 'identity.created', etc.
  issuer: string;            // DID of the actor
  subject: string;           // DID of the target
  scope: string;             // service scope ('events', 'pay', 'market', etc.)
  payload?: Record<string, unknown>;
  correlationId?: string;    // trace across reactors
  timestamp?: string;        // ISO 8601, defaults to now
}
```

### Reactor Chain Configuration

Phase 1: hardcoded defaults in `packages/bus/src/config.ts`. Same shape as the DB-backed
Phase 2 — so migration is mechanical, not architectural. Phase 3 adds scope-level overrides
(a community customizes its own reactor chains). Phase 4 is admin UI — "IFTTT for events."

We're building the config system in layers because over-building ahead of demand is how
projects die. One node, hardcoded config. When there are multiple nodes with different needs,
DB-backed. When community operators want self-service, scope overrides. Each phase is earned
by actual demand, not speculative architecture.

```typescript
// Example: listing.purchased chain
{
  'listing.purchased': [
    { type: 'attestation', config: { attestationType: 'listing.purchased' }, enabled: true },
    { type: 'mjn',       config: { attestationType: 'listing.purchased' }, enabled: true },
    { type: 'settle',    config: {}, await: true, enabled: true },
    { type: 'notify',    config: { scope: 'market:purchase' }, enabled: true },
  ],
}
```

Built-in reactors:
| Reactor | Purpose |
|---------|---------|
| `attestation` | Issues a signed attestation via auth service |
| `mjn` | Credits MJN tokens based on emission schedule |
| `settle` | Calls pay service to process .fair settlement |
| `notify` | Sends notification + interest signals |
| `emit` | Emits structured log/event for analytics |
| `dfos` | Phase 2: direct DFOS chain append (currently deferred to attestation path) |
| `webhook` | POSTs event payload to configured external URL |
| `project` | Release-gated projection reactor (§11 — control-plane pattern) |

### The listing.purchased Flow (Cleanest Example)

```typescript
// apps/market/app/api/webhook/route.ts
// Called by pay service after Stripe payment succeeds

bus.publish('listing.purchased', {
  issuer: body.metadata?.buyerDid || '',
  subject: listing.sellerDid,
  scope: 'market',
  payload: {
    context_id: listingId,
    context_type: 'market',
    amount: body.metadata?.amount || 0,
    currency: body.metadata?.currency || 'CAD',
    fairManifest: listing.fairManifest || null,
    funded: true,
    funded_provider: 'stripe',
    buyerDid: body.metadata?.buyerDid || '',
    metadata: { listingId },
    interestDids: [buyerDid, sellerDid].filter(Boolean),
  },
});
```

Chain execution:
1. **attestation reactor** → `emitAttestation({ issuer_did: buyer, subject_did: seller, type: 'listing.purchased', ... })`
2. **mjn reactor** → looks up `EMISSION_SCHEDULE['listing.purchased']`, credits buyer 0.25% of sale and seller 0.25% of sale in MJN
3. **settle reactor** → resolves .fair chain, deducts Stripe processing fee from seller share, POSTs to pay service `/api/settle`
4. **notify reactor** → sends `market:purchase` notification to seller, emits interest signals to both DIDs

### Broker Integration

`@imajin/bus` also exposes `bus.broker()` — the consent gate for selective disclosure (§8).
The bus and broker share the same package but are separate surfaces: `publish` moves events
through reactor chains; `broker()` evaluates a consent→scope→release→audit latch and returns
a filtered release envelope. Both are in-process; no HTTP round-trip.

### Reactor Registry

```typescript
import { registerReactor } from '@imajin/bus';

registerReactor('my-reactor', async (event, config) => {
  // Your side-effect logic
});
```

### Type-Safe Events

```typescript
import type { BusEventMap, BusEventType } from '@imajin/bus';

// Payloads are typed — the compiler catches bad call sites
await publish('identity.created', {
  issuer: did, subject: did, scope: 'auth',
  payload: { scope: 'actor', subtype: 'human', tier: 'soft', context_id: did, context_type: 'identity' }
});
```

---

## 6. Vault

The vault is an encrypted key-value store: seal a secret to the node's signing key from
anywhere, persist it as a CID-addressed blob, unseal only on the node. No plaintext at rest.
No SSH required. First production consumer: connector PATs (GitHub, Discord, QuickBooks).

**Custody disclosure:** The vault is **node-sealed (v1)** — secrets are encrypted with a key
derived from the node's `AUTH_PRIVATE_KEY`. The node operator *can technically decrypt*
stored secrets. The system is encrypted at rest and access-controlled, but this is NOT
zero-custody. Owner-sealed (zero-custody) storage is a tracked hardening milestone. See
SECURITY.md for the full honest-gaps list.

### Cryptography

- **AES-256-GCM** authenticated encryption (`node:crypto`)
- **HKDF-SHA256** seal-key derivation from the `AUTH_PRIVATE_KEY` Ed25519 seed — the sealing key is domain-separated from the signing key (salt `imajin-vault`, info `seal-v1`)
- **CID addressing** — every encrypted blob gets a deterministic `dag-cbor + SHA-256` CID

```typescript
import { sealSecret, unsealSecret, deriveSealKey } from '@imajin/vault-core';

// Derive the 32-byte seal key from the node's AUTH_PRIVATE_KEY
const keyBytes = deriveSealKey(process.env.AUTH_PRIVATE_KEY);

// Seal a plaintext string → { encrypted: base64(authTag||ciphertext), nonce: base64 }
const blob = sealSecret(plaintext, keyBytes);

// Unseal — throws on integrity failure (wrong key or tampered ciphertext)
const plaintext = unsealSecret(blob, keyBytes);
```

### Server-Callable Vault Service (`apps/kernel/src/lib/vault`)

```typescript
import { sealAndStore, loadAndUnseal } from '@/src/lib/vault';

// Seal and persist under a per-DID field name
await sealAndStore(`github-pat:${ownerDid}`, pat);

// Load, verify entry integrity, and unseal
const pat = await loadAndUnseal(`github-pat:${ownerDid}`);
// Returns undefined if not found; throws on integrity violation
```

Per-DID isolation is structural: the field name encodes the owner DID (`github-pat:${ownerDid}`),
so different DIDs cannot read each other's secrets. Cross-DID reads fail at the field-name gate
before any decryption is attempted.

### Why CIDs

Every encrypted blob gets a content-addressed identifier. You can verify integrity without
decrypting. Same addressing scheme as DFOS chains. CID chain per field = auditable rotation
history. Config references a CID, not a raw path — portable, content-verified.

### Bus Integration

Vault changes emit `vault.secret.updated` bus events. Services subscribe to the secrets they
consume and hot-reload on change — no restart, no deploy, no SSH. A token expires at 3am?
Rotate it from your phone. The service picks it up in seconds.

---

## 7. Content-Addressed Media Versioning

Every media asset has a content-addressed identity: a `dag-cbor + SHA-256` CID derived from
its canonical form. Editing a document is not an in-place mutation — it creates a new signed
version with a `previousCid` pointer, forming an auditable content chain.

### CID Chain per Asset

```
asset v1: cid = bafyrei...A  (no previousCid)
         ↓ update
asset v2: cid = bafyrei...B, previousCid = bafyrei...A
         ↓ update
asset v3: cid = bafyrei...C, previousCid = bafyrei...B
```

The current CID is stored in `assets.cid`. The full chain is walkable via `previousCid`
pointers. A consumer verifying content computes the CID from the payload and checks it
against the stored CID — no trust in the transport layer required.

### Per-DID Workspace Manifest

Each DID has a workspace manifest: a `.fair`-signed snapshot of all current asset CIDs owned
by that DID. The manifest enables:
- **Snapshot / rollback** — restore a previous state from the signed manifest
- **Branch** — fork the manifest to a new DID-owned workspace
- **Version badges** — UI surfaces the content chain depth as a version indicator

### Document-Changed Trigger

When `updateAssetContent()` persists a new content version, it:
1. Re-signs the asset as `.fair` (signed by `AUTH_PRIVATE_KEY` on-behalf-of the owner DID)
2. Publishes `document.changed` to the bus with `{ path, previousCid, newCid }`
3. The `project` reactor runs the release-gated projection pipeline (§11)

---

## 8. Selective Disclosure & Data Broker

This is the layer that makes the whole system worth building.

Every other identity system stores personal data in plaintext and hopes access control is
configured correctly. The broker inverts this: data is encrypted at rest, and only released
to specific requesters when the subject has granted consent for a specific purpose. Every
release is audited. Every denial is logged. The default state is locked.

The broker is **complete and in production**: consent grant CRUD, DB-backed consent reactor,
profile selective disclosure, HTTP broker API, and full audit trail are all shipped.

### The Problem It Solves

A traveler books a trip. They're vegetarian, have a shellfish allergy, prefer mid-range
restaurants, and have a Mastercard. The restaurant needs to know they're vegetarian. It does
NOT need to know their budget, their card number, or the specifics of their allergy. But in
every existing system, either the restaurant gets everything or nothing.

The broker gives you field-level consent with purpose-binding:

```json
{
  "dietary_restrictions": [
    { "purpose": "restaurant_reservation", "release": "raw", "duration": "trip" }
  ],
  "allergies": [
    { "purpose": "restaurant_reservation", "release": "attestation" },
    { "purpose": "emergency_medical", "release": "raw", "duration": "permanent" }
  ],
  "budget_preference": [
    { "purpose": "restaurant_reservation", "release": "none" }
  ]
}
```

The restaurant gets: dietary = "vegetarian, gluten-free" (raw). Allergies = "has allergies"
(attestation — enough to flag the kitchen, not enough to identify the condition). Budget =
not present in response. Every field, every purpose, every release mode is explicit.

### Release Modes

| Mode | What requester sees | When to use |
|------|---------------------|-------------|
| **raw** | Actual value: "vegetarian, gluten-free" | Explicit consent for this purpose |
| **attestation** | Confirmation only: `{ has_allergies: true }` | Awareness without exposure |
| **none** | Field omitted entirely | No consent, or explicitly denied |

### The Request Flow

```
Requester app           Imajin Broker             Subject's Data
    |                         |                         |
    | broker.request({        |                         |
    |   requester: ...,       |                         |
    |   subject: ...,         |                         |
    |   fields: [dietary,     |                         |
    |     allergies, budget], |                         |
    |   purpose: restaurant   |                         |
    | })                      |                         |
    |                         |                         |
    |                         | check consent grants    |
    |                         | dietary + restaurant    |
    |                         |   = raw ✅               |
    |                         | allergies + restaurant  |
    |                         |   = attestation ✅       |
    |                         | budget + restaurant     |
    |                         |   = none ❌              |
    |                         |                         |
    | <─ release envelope ── |                         |
    |   dietary: "vegetarian" |                         |
    |   allergies: {has:true} |                         |
    |   budget: [omitted]     |                         |
    |                         |                         |
    |   audit log written     |
```

Every request and release is logged with requester DID, subject DID, fields requested, fields
released, fields denied, purpose, and timestamp. The subject can see their full audit trail.

### HTTP Broker API

```
POST /broker/api/request
{ requester: did, subject: did, fields: [...], purpose: "..." }
→ { released: { field: value | { attestation: true } }, denied: [...] }

POST /broker/api/consent
{ grantorDid: did, grantedTo: did, field: "dietary_restrictions", purpose: "...", release: "raw" }
→ { consentId: "..." }

DELETE /broker/api/consent/{consentId}
→ 204
```

### Broker Audit Log

Every consent decision writes a `broker_audit_log` row (consent→scope→release→audit latch).
The subject can query their full history: who asked for what, what was released, what was denied.

---

## 9. Attestations

Attestations are how trust is built. Not granted by an admin, not assumed by default —
earned through signed, verifiable claims. "Alice vouched for Bob." "Bob bought a ticket."
"The platform settled a $150 transaction with a verified .fair chain." Each of these is an
attestation: a signed JSON object with an issuer, a subject, a type, and a payload.

Unsigned attestations are rejected at write — a 4xx, not a null signature. If you can't
sign it, it didn't happen.

### Shape

The essential fields: `issuerDid`, `subjectDid`, `type`, `payload`, `signature`, `cid`.
Countersigning (`witnessJws`) transitions status from `pending` → `bilateral` — both
parties have now signed. Multi-party documents use `collecting` → `executed` with
`totalSigners` tracking.

### Attestation Types (Live)

```typescript
const ATTESTATION_TYPES = [
  'transaction.settled', 'customer', 'connection.invited', 'connection.accepted',
  'vouch', 'session.created', 'identity.created', 'identity.verified.preliminary',
  'identity.verified.hard', 'identity.verified.steward', 'identity.verified.operator',
  'tip.granted', 'ticket.purchased', 'listing.purchased', 'group.created',
  'group.member.added', 'group.member.removed', 'group.member.left',
  'pod.created', 'pod.member.added', 'pod.member.removed', 'pod.role.changed',
  'scope.onboard', 'event.created', 'event.attendance', 'handle.claimed',
  'learn.enrolled', 'learn.completed', 'stub.created', 'asset.fair.upgraded',
  'document.created', 'document.signed', 'document.executed', 'document.declined',
  'app.authorized', 'asset.article.published',
  'github.issue.created', 'github.comment.created',
] as const;
```

### Signing

Sign the canonicalized JSON of:
```json
{
  "subject_did": "did:imajin:subject...",
  "type": "connection.invited",
  "context_id": "inv_abc123",
  "context_type": "connection",
  "payload": { "delivery": "link" },
  "issued_at": 1742900000000
}
```

`canonicalize()` sorts keys deterministically. `issued_at` is Unix milliseconds. Result is 128-char hex Ed25519 signature.

### Creating an Attestation

```
POST /auth/api/attestations
Cookie: imajin_session=...
{
  "issuer_did": "did:imajin:issuer...",
  "subject_did": "did:imajin:subject...",
  "type": "vouch",
  "context_id": "inv_abc123",
  "context_type": "connection",
  "payload": { "note": "great colleague" },
  "signature": "deadbeef...128-char-hex...",
  "issued_at": 1742900000000
}
```

Internal (server-side) attestations are signed by the platform keypair (`AUTH_PRIVATE_KEY`):
```
POST /auth/api/attestations/internal
Authorization: Bearer {ATTESTATION_INTERNAL_API_KEY}
{ issuer_did, subject_did, type, context_id?, context_type?, payload? }
```

### Querying

```
GET /auth/api/attestations?subject_did={did}&type=vouch&limit=20
GET /auth/api/attestations/{did}   // shorthand
```

### Countersigning

```
POST /auth/api/attestations/countersign
{ "attestationId": "att_abc", "witnessJws": "<jws>" }
```
Transitions status from `pending` → `bilateral`. Only the subject can countersign.

---

## 10. .fair Attribution

Every asset carries a `.fair` manifest: who contributed, in what proportion, under what terms.
This is the answer to "who gets paid?" — not as a policy document, but as a machine-readable
fee schedule that the settle reactor executes automatically.

The manifest travels WITH the content. Not stored separately. Not in a different system.
When money moves, the manifest says where it goes. The bus reactor reads the manifest and
splits the payment. No human intervention. No invoice. No accounts receivable.

### Manifest Format (v1.1)

```typescript
interface FairManifestV1_1 {
  fair: "1.1";
  version: "1.1";
  id: string;
  type: string;
  owner: string;               // DID
  created: string;             // ISO 8601
  source?: string;
  access: FairAccessV1_1 | "public" | "private";
  transfer?: FairTransferV1_1;
  fees?: FairFee[];
  attribution: DidShareList;   // who gets paid
  distribution?: {             // usage rights
    reproduction?: FairDistributionRight;
    streaming?: FairDistributionRight;
    derivative?: FairDistributionRight;
    syndication?: FairDistributionRight;
  };
  training?: FairTraining;
  commercial?: FairCommercial;
  integrity?: FairIntegrity;
  terms?: string;
  intent?: FairIntent;
  signature?: Signature;       // Ed25519, base64url
  tipping?: { enabled: boolean };
  settlement?: SettlementConfig;
  chain?: DidShareList;        // backward compat alias
  distributions?: DidShareList; // backward compat alias
}
```

### Fee Cascade

```typescript
import { buildFairManifest } from '@imajin/fair';

const manifest = buildFairManifest({
  creatorDid: "did:imajin:alice...",
  contentDid: "did:imajin:asset_xxx",
  contentType: "track",
  scopeDid: null,
  nodeOperatorDid: "did:imajin:node...",
  nodeFeeBps: 50,          // 0.5% — clamped to [25, 200]
  buyerCreditBps: 25,      // 0.25% — clamped to [25, 200]
});
```

Fee cascade — deducted in order, seller gets the remainder:
1. **Protocol fee**: 1.0% (fixed — this is how the network sustains itself)
2. **Node fee**: 0.5% default, operator-configurable [0.25%, 2.0%]
3. **Buyer credit**: 0.25% default — the buyer earns MJN for transacting
4. **Scope fee**: optional — only when a community/business takes a cut
5. **Seller share**: everything else

Default total overhead: 2%. The seller gets 98% of what reaches the protocol. Compare to
Stripe's 2.9% + $0.30 where the platform keeps everything. Here, the 2% is split across four
stakeholders who all contributed to the transaction happening.

**Note on processor fees:** Imajin's 2% is *additive* to the underlying payment processor's fee,
not a replacement for it. A Stripe-settled transaction still incurs Stripe's 2.9% + $0.30; the
Imajin protocol fee is layered on top and split across stakeholders. The comparison above is
about *who keeps the overhead* (one platform vs. four contributors), not about eliminating
processor costs.

Chain placeholder DIDs:
- `BUYER_PLACEHOLDER` → resolved to actual buyer DID at settlement
- `NODE_PLACEHOLDER` → resolved to `NODE_DID` env var

### Settlement via Bus Reactor

The `settleReactor` (`packages/bus/src/reactors/settle.ts`):
1. Extracts amount, currency, fairManifest from event payload
2. Resolves placeholder DIDs in the chain
3. Deducts estimated Stripe processing fee from seller share
4. Fixes rounding drift by adjusting the seller entry
5. POSTs to pay service `/api/settle` with resolved chain

```typescript
// The settle reactor POSTs this body to pay service:
{
  from_did: buyerDid,
  total_amount: 14.63,        // dollars after fee deduction
  service: "market",
  type: "listing.purchased",
  funded: true,
  funded_provider: "stripe",
  fair_manifest: {
    chain: [
      { did: "did:imajin:protocol...", amount: 0.15, role: "protocol" },
      { did: "did:imajin:node...", amount: 0.07, role: "node" },
      { did: "did:imajin:buyer...", amount: 0.04, role: "buyer_credit" },
      { did: "did:imajin:seller...", amount: 14.37, role: "seller" },
    ]
  }
}
```

---

## 11. Control-Plane Pattern: Scope-Manifest → Release-Gated Projection

This is the pattern by which human-authored signed documents govern what connectors and
agents can do. The human edits a document they own; the system reads it and enforces it.
No admin panel. No API call. Grant by editing. Revoke by deleting a line.

### The Pattern

A **scope-manifest** is a `.fair`-signed media asset with `metadata.kind: scope-manifest`.
It declares which scopes a connector is permitted to use, with a `release:` header per scope
derived from the 2×2 consent taxonomy (§11.4):

```markdown
---
connector: did:imajin:github-connector
channel: github
---

## github:read
release: silent
verb: read
surface: repos/issues/PRs
label: Read your repos and issues

## github:write
release: on-consent
verb: write
surface: issues/PRs
label: Open and comment on issues and PRs
```

### Release-Gated Projection Reactor (`project` reactor)

On `document.changed`, the `project` reactor:
1. Reads the authored document at `payload.path`
2. Parses each field's `release:` header via `parseReleasePolicy()`
3. For each declared field, calls the broker's consent→scope→release→audit latch
4. **Released fields** are passed to registered `ProjectionSurface.apply()` implementations
5. **Gated/removed fields** trigger `ProjectionSurface.remove()` — de-materializing the row and firing downstream revocation

The broker's audit reactor writes a `broker_audit_log` row for every consent-gated decision.

### Projection Surfaces

Surfaces plug in via `registerProjectionSurface()` — adding a new connector never touches
the reactor core:

```typescript
import { registerProjectionSurface } from '@/src/lib/media/projection-reactor';

registerProjectionSurface({
  name: 'channel-links',
  async apply(ctx, released) {
    // Upsert an active auth.channel_links row for each released scope
  },
  async remove(ctx, removedFields) {
    // Flip status → 'revoked' + publish channel.link.revoked
  },
});
```

### Grant / Revoke Flow

```
Human edits scope-manifest (adds github:write line)
  → updateAssetContent() re-signs the .fair asset
  → document.changed published to bus
  → project reactor runs broker latch for github:write
  → consent grant exists → RELEASED
  → channel-links surface upserts active auth.channel_links row
  → GitHub connector can now call github:write surface

Human deletes github:write line from manifest
  → document.changed published
  → project reactor: github:write not in declared fields → REMOVED
  → channel-links surface: status → 'revoked', publishes channel.link.revoked
  → GitHub connector physically cannot reach github:write surface
```

Delete = revoke. The file (signed) is the authority. No separate revocation API call needed.

### 2×2 Scope Taxonomy

```
                  read        write
only-you         silent      itemized / on-consent
touches-others   on-consent  deliberate (never silent)
```

A connector scope is classified by `(read|write) × (only-you|touches-others)`. The
`touches-others` column can never be `silent` — this falls out of the taxonomy and is
enforced by `deriveReleaseTier()`, not hand-assigned per scope.

---

## 12. MCP Connector Surface

The kernel exposes a sovereign connector door at `mcp.imajin.ai`. Claude Desktop and any
MCP-compatible client can connect to it. The MCP surface is the agent-protocol-interop
entry point: a human's Imajin identity plus their consented scope-manifests determine
exactly what the connected agent can do.

### OAuth 2.1 Authorization Server

The MCP surface implements a full OAuth 2.1 AS (RFC 8414 discovery, RFC 7591 DCR, RFC 7636
PKCE, RFC 8707 resource indicators):

```
GET  /.well-known/openid-configuration   → RFC 8414 AS metadata
GET  /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource → RFC 9728 protected-resource metadata
POST /oauth/register                     → RFC 7591 Dynamic Client Registration
GET  /oauth/authorize                    → authorization code flow (PKCE required)
POST /oauth/token                        → code → access token + refresh token
POST /mcp                                → JSON-RPC 2.0 tool dispatch
```

**Dynamic Client Registration (DCR):** Claude Desktop requires DCR — it rejects an AS with
no `registration_endpoint`. Registration is accepted ONLY for exact Anthropic callback URIs
(`claude.ai/api/mcp/auth_callback`, `claude.com/api/mcp/auth_callback`). No wildcards.
A registered client is inert until a real DID consents.

**Token lifetimes:** access token 10 minutes, authorization code 60 seconds (single-use),
refresh token 90 days.

### Per-DID Scope Gate

Every `tools/call` JSON-RPC invocation is checked against the caller's granted scopes:
```
tool.requiredScope && !ctx.scopes.has(tool.requiredScope)
→ { isError: true, content: [{ text: "insufficient_scope" }] }
```

### MCP Tools Registry

Tools are registered in `apps/kernel/src/lib/mcp/tools/index.ts`. Adding a new tool is a
single entry in the registry — the `/mcp` route handler and JSON-RPC dispatch never change.

| Tool | Required scope | Purpose |
|------|---------------|---------|
| `media_list` | `media:read` | List the caller's media assets |
| `media_get` | `media:read` | Get a single asset |
| `media_write` | `media:write` | Create or update a media asset |
| `media_share` | `media:share` | Add/remove allowedDids on an asset |
| `connections_list` | `connections:read` | List trust-graph connections |
| `github_create_issue` | `github:write` | Create a GitHub issue on behalf of DID |
| `github_create_comment` | `github:write` | Create a GitHub issue comment |
| `github_list_issues` | `github:read` | List issues in a repo |
| `github_get_issue` | `github:read` | Get a single issue |
| `github_connect` | `github:write` | Seal a GitHub PAT in the vault |
| `discord_send_message` | `discord:write` | Send a Discord message |
| `discord_connect` | `discord:write` | Seal a Discord bot token in the vault |

### Scope Vocabulary (MCP ceiling)

Scopes in `MCP_SCOPES` and `packages/auth/src/scopes.ts`:

```
media:read, media:write, media:share, connections:read
github:read (silent), github:write (on-consent)
github:org (on-consent — touches others), github:actions (never)
discord:read (silent), discord:write (on-consent)
```

---

## 13. Connectors

Connectors are external integrations registered as actor DIDs, with credentials sealed in
the vault and scopes governed by the owner's scope-manifest (§11). The pattern is uniform:
connector DID + scope-manifest + vault-sealed credential + MCP tools.

### GitHub Connector

- **Connector DID:** `did:imajin:github-connector`
- **Credential:** fine-grained GitHub PAT, sealed in the vault under `github-pat:${ownerDid}`
- **Scope fixture:** `apps/kernel/src/lib/media/__tests__/fixtures/github-scope-manifest.md`
- **Tools:** `github_create_issue`, `github_create_comment`, `github_list_issues`, `github_get_issue`, `github_connect`
- **Attribution:** work is owner-pinned to the human's DID (authorship accrues to the human, not the connector)
- **Bus events:** `github.issue.created`, `github.comment.created` (non-fatal, post-action)
- **Fail-closed:** no active `channel_links` row OR no sealed PAT → throw, no GitHub API call

### Discord Connector

- **Connector DID:** `did:imajin:discord-connector`
- **Credential:** Discord bot token, sealed in vault under `discord-token:${ownerDid}`
- **Tools:** `discord_send_message`, `discord_connect`
- **Same vault/scope-manifest/MCP pattern as GitHub connector**

### QuickBooks Connector

- **Per-DID config:** OAuth-based, config stored per DID
- **Capabilities:** invoice write/read, settlement integration
- **Tools:** registered in the kernel `/quickbooks/*` route group
- **Settlement:** integrates with the .fair settlement reactor for invoice flows

### Adding a New Connector

1. Register a connector DID (`did:imajin:<name>-connector`)
2. Add scope vocabulary to `packages/auth/src/scopes.ts` and `mcp/oauth-config.ts`
3. Create a backend lib in `apps/kernel/src/lib/<name>/connector.ts` — seal/unseal credentials via `sealAndStore`/`loadAndUnseal`, resolve active grant via `channel_links`, act `onBehalfOf` the human's DID
4. Register a `ProjectionSurface` for `channel_links` management — do NOT modify `projection-reactor.ts` core
5. Add MCP tools to `mcp/tools/<name>.ts` and register in `ALL_TOOLS`
6. Create a `.fair`-signed scope-manifest fixture under `__tests__/fixtures/`

---

## 14. Intention Inference Engine

The inference engine is the "you do a human thing" surface. A human sends a voice note,
takes a photo, or drops a file. The engine infers intent, routes to the right vocabulary
of actions, and proposes what to do next. The human confirms before anything crosses a
boundary. The engine signs a record of what it inferred and what was authorized.

### Pipeline

```
Gesture (voice/photo/file/text)
  → captureGesture(): DID-pinned asset + inference_sessions row + DFOS capture event
  → gatherContext(): resolve identity, connections, recent assets, calendar state
  → classifyIntent(): vocabulary-mounted LLM routing (intent → action candidates)
  → resolvePolicy(): consent + scope + release latch — inferred action validated
  → propose / execute: human confirms, engine executes on their behalf
```

### Capture Surface

```typescript
import { captureGesture } from '@/src/lib/inference/capture';

const captureEvent = await captureGesture({
  buffer,            // raw audio/image/file bytes
  filename,          // original filename
  mimeType,          // audio/* → voice, image/* → photo, text/* → text, else file
  ownerDid,
  vocabularyName,    // mounted vocabulary — governs intent classification routing
});
// → { sessionId, assetId, kind, ownerDid }
```

Captures are always `access: private` until explicitly shared. `inference.capture` DFOS
event published (best-effort, non-fatal).

### Vocabulary Mount

A **vocabulary** is a named set of action templates. Mounting a vocabulary tells the engine
what actions are available for this gesture source. The vocabulary name is stored on the
`inference_sessions` row and drives LLM routing.

### Session Lifecycle

```
status: capturing → classifying → proposing → executing → completed | failed
```

The session ID chains the entire pipeline from raw gesture to executed action. Every
transition is signed and auditable.

---

## 15. Calendar

The calendar primitive provides broker-gated temporal scheduling. Availability is not
exposed by default — it is released only to requesters with an explicit consent grant
scoped to the purpose and relationship class.

### Route Surface

```
GET  /calendar/api/availability/{did}   → broker-gated availability slots
POST /calendar/api/entries              → create a temporal entry
GET  /calendar/api/entries              → list own entries (requires auth)
```

### Relationship-Class Consent

Calendar consent grants carry a `relationship_class`:
- `personal` — release to explicit personal connections only
- `professional` — release to business-context requesters
- `public` — release to any DID with a consent grant for this purpose

The broker latch checks relationship class before releasing any slot data.

---

## 16. Settlement

Money comes in through wire schemes (Stripe, Interac e-Transfer, eventually Solana Pay,
Lightning). The .fair manifest says where it goes. The settle reactor does the math. The
MJNx ledger tracks internal balances. This is plumbing — intentionally boring, because
boring plumbing is reliable plumbing.

### Stripe Integration

The pay service creates Stripe Checkout sessions:

```
POST /pay/api/checkout
{
  "items": [{ "name": "Jacket", "amount": 15000, "quantity": 1 }],
  "currency": "CAD",
  "successUrl": "...",
  "cancelUrl": "...",
  "metadata": { "service": "market", "listingId": "lst_abc", "sellerDid": "...", "buyerDid": "..." },
  "fairManifest": { ... },
  "sellerDid": "did:imajin:seller..."
}
→ { "id": "cs_live_xxx", "url": "...", "transactionId": "tx_abc" }
```

Seller DID lookup: if seller has a connected Stripe account (`chargesEnabled: true`), the session routes through Connect with platform fee applied.

### MJNx Ledger

MJN is the internal credit token. 1 MJN = $0.01 (1¢). 100 MJN = 1 MJNx.

The protocol doesn't require the token. The token requires the protocol. MJNx is to Imajin
as USD is to SWIFT. SWIFT moves money, it doesn't BE money. The token is an accounting unit
for the node's internal ledger, not a speculative asset.

Emissions are triggered by the MJN reactor (`packages/bus/src/reactors/mjn.ts`):

```typescript
// EMISSION_SCHEDULE excerpt:
'listing.purchased': {
  gas: 0.01,
  emit: [
    { to: 'subject', amount: '0.25%', reason: 'Purchase reward' },
    { to: 'issuer', amount: '0.25%', reason: 'Sale reward' },
  ],
}
```

The reactor resolves percentage amounts against the settlement value and POSTs credits to pay service `/api/emission`:
```typescript
{
  to_did: targetDid,
  amount: 2.5,               // MJN
  currency: 'MJN',
  reason: 'Purchase reward',
  metadata: { attestation_type: 'listing.purchased', ... }
}
```

### Settlement Schemes

Supported schemes: `x402 | stripe-link | mjnx-direct | solana-pay | lightning`

Configured per manifest in `settlement.schemes`.

---

## 17. Chat

DID-based messaging. Every conversation is a DID.

### Conversation DIDs

```typescript
import { dmDid, groupDid, parseConversationDid } from '@/src/lib/chat/conversation-did';

const did = dmDid(didA, didB);     // deterministic, sorted
const gdid = groupDid([didA, didB, didC]); // deterministic, sorted

parseConversationDid('did:imajin:dm:abc123');
// → { type: 'dm', slug: 'abc123' }
```

### REST API

```
POST /chat/api/d/{conversationDid}/messages
Cookie: imajin_session=...
{
  "content": { "text": "Hello!" },
  "contentType": "text",
  "replyToMessageId": "msg_abc"
}
→ { message: { id, conversationDid, fromDid, content, contentType, signature, createdAt, reactions: [] } }
```

```
GET /chat/api/d/{conversationDid}/messages?limit=50&before=msg_abc
→ { messages: [...], hasMore: true }
```

Requires tier != `soft`. Access verified via auth service.

### Message Types

```typescript
export type MessageContent =
  | { type?: "text"; text: string; encrypted?: string; nonce?: string; meta?: MessageMeta }
  | { type: "voice"; assetId: string; transcript: string; durationMs: number; waveform?: number[] }
  | { type: "media"; assetId: string; filename: string; mimeType: string; size: number; width?: number; height?: number; caption?: string }
  | { type: "location"; lat: number; lng: number; label?: string; accuracy?: number }
  | { type: "system"; event: "member_added" | "member_removed" | "member_left" | "created" | "renamed"; actorDid: string; targetDid?: string; occurredAt?: string; attestationId?: string };
```

### WebSocket Protocol

Connect to `wss://chat.imajin.ai/ws`.

**Authentication:**
- Cookie-based (automatic): session cookie on upgrade request
- Token-based (deferred): connect without cookie, receive `{ type: "auth_required" }`, send `{ type: "auth", token: "..." }`

WS tokens are short-lived (30 seconds, one-time use):
```
POST /chat/api/ws-token
Cookie: imajin_session=...
→ { token: "wst_xxx" }
```

**Client → Server:**
```json
{ "type": "subscribe", "conversationId": "did:imajin:dm:abc123" }
{ "type": "typing", "conversationId": "...", "name": "Alice" }
{ "type": "stop_typing", "conversationId": "..." }
{ "type": "ping" }
```

**Server → Client:**
```json
{ "type": "connected" }
{ "type": "new_message", "message": { ... } }
{ "type": "message_updated", "message": { ... } }
{ "type": "user_typing", "conversationId": "...", "did": "...", "name": "Bob" }
{ "type": "user_presence", "did": "...", "online": true, "lastSeen": null }
{ "type": "pong" }
```

Presence tracked per-DID (multiple tabs share a DID; last tab closing triggers offline).

### Chat encryption (Simplified)

> **Maturity note:** This is transport/payload encryption with a key-epoch model, **not** a
> ratcheting forward-secret E2EE protocol (no forward secrecy or post-compromise security yet).
> Do not treat it as equivalent to Signal-class messaging. Full ratcheting E2EE (MLS / Double
> Ratchet) is planned — see #458.

Chat uses X25519 key agreement + XChaCha20-Poly1305:

```typescript
import {
  generateKeyPair, deriveSharedSecret,
  encryptMessage, decryptMessage
} from '@/src/lib/chat/crypto';

const kp = generateKeyPair();
const shared = deriveSharedSecret(privateKey, peerPublicKey);
const { ciphertext, nonce } = encryptMessage(plaintext, shared);
const plaintext = decryptMessage(ciphertext, nonce, shared);
```

Group chats use a shared group key re-encrypted to each member's X25519 public key.

---

## 18. Connections & Trust Graph

Pods are the unit of trust. Two people in a pod are connected. Connections form a graph.
Trust is measured in hops. This is how the system knows "Alice is 2 hops from Bob" without
either of them being in the same group — Alice shares a pod with Carol, Carol shares a pod
with Bob.

Pod types: `personal` (default, private), `shared`, `event` (auto-created for event attendees).
Pod members have roles: `owner | admin | member`.

### Trust Distance

BFS shortest path in hops between two DIDs through pod co-membership:

```typescript
import { trustDistance, trustRadius, resolvePodMembers } from '@imajin/trust-graph';

const hops = await trustDistance(db, didA, didB, maxHops = 6);
// Returns -1 if no path found

const radius = await trustRadius(db, didA, maxHops = 3);
// Returns Set of all DIDs within N hops

const members = await resolvePodMembers(db, podId, maxDepth = 3);
// Recursively resolves members from linked child pods
```

### Invites

```
POST /connections/api/invites
{ "delivery": "link", "note": "...", "maxUses": 1 }
→ { invite: { id, code, url, ... } }
```

Link invite limits by role: admin (unlimited), legendary (10), trusted (5), member (3), newbie (1).

---

## 19. Agent Identity

Agents are first-class identities, not API keys. An agent has a DID, a keypair, a trust
history, and scoped delegation to act on behalf of its principal. It authenticates the same
way a human does (challenge-response). It builds reputation the same way (attestations).
The system doesn't care if you're carbon or silicon — it cares if you can sign.

### Creating an Agent

```
POST /auth/api/agents
Cookie: imajin_session=...
{ "handle": "my-agent", "name": "My Agent", "displayName": "Helper" }
→ { agent: { did, handle, name, publicKey, tier: 'preliminary' } }
```

This:
1. Generates an Ed25519 keypair for the agent
2. Creates an `identities` row with `subtype: 'agent'`, `scope: 'actor'`
3. Creates an `identity_members` row linking the agent to the caller with role `agent`

### Listing Agents

```
GET /auth/api/agents
→ { agents: [{ did, handle, displayName, name, tier, status, role }] }
```

### Agent Challenge-Response

Same as human challenge-response, but the agent's DID is used:
```
POST /auth/api/challenge
{ "did": "did:imajin:agent_xxx" }
→ { challengeId, challenge, expiresAt }

POST /auth/api/authenticate
{ "challengeId": "...", "signature": "..." }
```

---

## 20. DFOS Integration

### Relay

DFOS relay runs at `registry.imajin.ai/relay/`. Every node runs an embedded relay.
DFOS relay 0.13.5: `/proof/v1` route surface, 133/133 conformance tests passing.

### Bridge

The `@imajin/dfos` package bridges Imajin Ed25519 keypairs to DFOS identity chains:

```typescript
import { createIdentityChain, updateIdentityChain, verifyChain } from '@imajin/dfos';

// Create chain
const { did, log, operationCID } = await createIdentityChain({ privateKeyHex, publicKeyHex });

// Verify chain
const verified = await verifyChain(log);
// → { did, authKeys: [...], assertKeys: [...], controllerKeys: [...], createdAt, updatedAt }
```

### Content Publishing

```typescript
import { publishContentEvent, getContentEvent } from '@imajin/dfos';

const event = await publishContentEvent({
  privateKeyHex,
  publicKeyHex,
  manifest: fairManifest,
  contentCid: 'bafyrei...',
});
```

---

## 21. Integration Patterns

### Adding a New Userspace App

Every new app follows the same pattern:

```typescript
import { requireAuth, requireHardDID, requireAppAuth } from '@imajin/auth';
import { publish } from '@imajin/bus';

// 1. Auth guard
const authResult = await requireAuth(request, { service: 'myapp' });
if ('error' in authResult) { return Response.json({ error: ... }, { status: ... }); }

// 2. Emit business event
await publish('myapp.action', {
  issuer: identity.id,
  subject: targetDid,
  scope: 'myapp',
  payload: { context_id: resourceId, context_type: 'myapp', ... }
});
```

### Environment Variables

```
AUTH_SERVICE_URL=https://jin.imajin.ai/auth   (kernel path, not separate service)
PAY_SERVICE_URL=https://jin.imajin.ai/pay
CHAT_SERVICE_URL=https://jin.imajin.ai/chat
ATTESTATION_INTERNAL_API_KEY=...
AUTH_PRIVATE_KEY=64-char-hex...
NODE_DID=did:imajin:node_xxx
MCP_PUBLIC_URL=https://mcp.imajin.ai
```

### Cross-Service Calls

Forward the session cookie:
```typescript
const response = await fetch(`${CHAT_SERVICE_URL}/api/d/${did}/messages`, {
  headers: { Cookie: request.headers.get('cookie') || '' }
});
```

### Database Pattern

Each service defines its schema in `src/db/schema.ts` using Drizzle ORM. The kernel uses per-service schemas:

```typescript
import { pgSchema } from 'drizzle-orm/pg-core';
export const eventsSchema = pgSchema('events');
export const marketSchema = pgSchema('market');
```

Shared DB connection via `@imajin/db`:
```typescript
import { createDb } from '@imajin/db';
const db = createDb(schema);
```

**Circular dependency constraint:** `packages/bus` MUST NOT import `apps/kernel`. The bus
package is a shared substrate; the kernel consumes it. Any kernel-side reactor logic lives
in `apps/kernel/src/lib/*/` and self-registers via `registerReactor()` /
`registerProjectionSurface()` from `@imajin/bus`.

---

## 22. Cryptographic Stack

| Primitive | Purpose |
|-----------|---------|
| Ed25519 | DID keypairs, attestation signing, .fair signing, session auth, vault identity signing |
| SHA-256 | Content hashing, hard DID derivation, HKDF operations |
| HKDF-SHA256 | Vault seal-key derivation from Ed25519 seed (domain-separated from signing key) |
| AES-256-GCM | Vault secret encryption (node-sealed, authenticated) |
| X25519 | Chat key agreement (derived from Ed25519) |
| XChaCha20-Poly1305 | Chat message encryption |
| dag-cbor + CID | DFOS content addressing, attestation CIDs, asset versioning |
| JWS | DFOS signature format for chain operations |
| JWT | Session cookies (HS256, signed by auth service) |
| PKCE S256 | OAuth 2.1 authorization code protection (MCP connector) |

Key encodings:
- Ed25519 private key: 32-byte seed, hex-encoded (64 chars) or PKCS#8 DER (96 chars)
- Ed25519 public key: 32-byte point, hex-encoded (64 chars)
- Ed25519 signature: 64 bytes, hex-encoded (128 chars)
- DFOS keys also in multibase (`z` prefix, base58btc)

The same Ed25519 keypair produces both `did:imajin:xxx` and `did:dfos:xxx`. Byte-compatible. Also a valid Solana wallet address.

---

## 23. Quick Reference

- Source: github.com/ima-jin/imajin-ai
- Kernel: jin.imajin.ai (consolidated auth/pay/chat/media/profile/connections/registry/mcp)
- MCP connector: mcp.imajin.ai
- DFOS relay: registry.imajin.ai/relay/
- OpenAPI specs: https://jin.imajin.ai/api/spec (kernel), https://{app}.imajin.ai/api/spec (userspace)

### Common curl Flows

```bash
# 1. Soft DID session
curl -c cookies.txt -X POST https://jin.imajin.ai/auth/api/session/soft \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"email":"user@example.com","name":"Jane"}'

# 2. Get session
curl -b cookies.txt https://jin.imajin.ai/auth/api/session

# 3. Challenge for keypair auth
curl -X POST https://jin.imajin.ai/auth/api/challenge \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"handle":"alice"}'

# 4. Authenticate with signature
curl -c cookies.txt -X POST https://jin.imajin.ai/auth/api/authenticate \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"challengeId":"chl_xxx","signature":"deadbeef..."}'

# 5. Create attestation
curl -b cookies.txt -X POST https://jin.imajin.ai/auth/api/attestations \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"issuer_did":"did:imajin:...","subject_did":"did:imajin:...","type":"vouch","signature":"..."}'

# 6. Send message
curl -b cookies.txt -X POST "https://jin.imajin.ai/chat/api/d/did:imajin:dm:abc123/messages" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"content":{"text":"Hello!"},"contentType":"text"}'

# 7. Get WS token
curl -b cookies.txt -X POST https://jin.imajin.ai/chat/api/ws-token
→ { "token": "wst_xxx" }

# 8. Broker consent grant
curl -b cookies.txt -X POST https://jin.imajin.ai/broker/api/consent \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"grantorDid":"did:imajin:...","grantedTo":"did:imajin:restaurant...","field":"dietary_restrictions","purpose":"restaurant_reservation","release":"raw"}'

# 9. MCP OAuth discovery
curl https://mcp.imajin.ai/.well-known/openid-configuration

# 10. Create invite
curl -b cookies.txt -X POST https://jin.imajin.ai/connections/api/invites \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"delivery":"link"}'
```

---

# END OF DOCUMENT
